title: Module Check: Tree and Graph Traversal — BFS/DFS Duality

questions.question: In DFS three-colour marking, what do white, gray, and black mean, and how does encountering a gray node prove a cycle?
questions.answer: White = unvisited, gray = on the current call stack, black = fully processed. A gray node means we reached an ancestor still in the current DFS path — a back edge — which proves a directed cycle.

questions.question: Why does BFS guarantee shortest path in an unweighted graph, and why does DFS not?
questions.answer: BFS explores all nodes at distance d before any at d+1, so the first time it reaches a node is via the minimum number of edges. DFS may reach a node via a longer path first, then never revisit it.

questions.question: After running Kahn's algorithm, len(result) < n. What does this reveal and why?
questions.answer: The graph contains a directed cycle. Nodes in a cycle never have their in-degree reach zero, so they are never enqueued. If result is shorter than the node count, the missing nodes are part of a cycle.

questions.question: Which traversal order processes all children before their parent node?
questions.explanation: Post-order visits left subtree → right subtree → root. This means children are fully processed before the parent is visited — required for problems like tree height, diameter, and serialisation.

questions.question: In an adjacency list representation, what is the time complexity of iterating all neighbours of every node across the full graph?
questions.explanation: Visiting each node is O(V); iterating all edges (once per endpoint) is O(E). Together O(V+E) is the standard BFS/DFS complexity on an adjacency list. O(V²) would apply to an adjacency matrix.
